questions.questionText: The most commonly used intravenous induction agent in modern anesthesia is:
questions.optionB: Propofol (rapid onset 30-40 seconds, smooth induction, antiemetic property, brief duration due to redistribution)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Propofol is the most widely used IV induction agent. Why correct: Propofol — rapid onset (30-40 sec), smooth induction, rapid recovery (redistribution), antiemetic properties, pleasant emergence. GABA-A receptor agonist. Why A wrong: Thiopentone (barbiturate) — was standard before propofol. Slower recovery, hangover effect, contraindicated in porphyria. Why C wrong: Ketamine — dissociative agent, used for hemodynamically unstable patients (maintains BP/HR), causes emergence delirium. Why D wrong: Etomidate — minimal cardiovascular depression (best for unstable patients), adrenal suppression with prolonged use. Clinical relevance: Propofol infusion syndrome (PRIS): rare but fatal with prolonged infusion — metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, cardiac failure. Exam trap: Propofol causes dose-dependent hypotension and apnea — titrate carefully. It is formulated in soybean oil (lipid emulsion), egg lecithin → avoid in egg/soy allergy. Memory aid: 'Propofol = Prompt, Pleasant, Popular induction agent.' Reference: Aitkenhead – IV Anesthetics.

questions.questionText: Malignant hyperthermia during anesthesia is triggered by:
questions.optionB: Succinylcholine and volatile anesthetic agents (halothane, sevoflurane, isoflurane — mutation in ryanodine receptor → uncontrolled calcium release)
questions.explanation: Core concept: MH is triggered by succinylcholine + volatile agents in genetically susceptible individuals. Why correct: Autosomal dominant mutation in RYR1 (ryanodine receptor) → uncontrolled skeletal muscle calcium release → sustained contraction → massive heat production, rigidity, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis. Why A wrong: Propofol is SAFE in MH — used as alternative IV agent. Why C wrong: Opioids don't trigger MH. Why D wrong: Benzodiazepines are safe. Clinical relevance: Treatment: DANTROLENE (blocks calcium release from SR) — specific antidote. Stop triggering agents, active cooling, hyperventilate, treat hyperkalemia. Mortality without treatment ~80%. Exam trap: Earliest sign of MH = unexplained RISE IN ETCO2 + tachycardia (not always immediate rigidity). Temperature rise may be late. Memory aid: 'Malignant Hyperthermia = Muscular catastrophe → Dantrolene = Drug of choice.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Complications of Anesthesia.

questions.questionText: Minimum Alveolar Concentration (MAC) of an inhalational anesthetic is defined as:
questions.optionB: Alveolar concentration at which 50% of patients do NOT move in response to surgical incision (measure of anesthetic potency — lower MAC = more potent)
questions.explanation: Core concept: MAC = ED50 for immobility to surgical stimulus = measure of potency. Why correct: MAC is the alveolar concentration preventing movement in 50% of subjects to a standard surgical incision. Lower MAC = MORE potent agent. MAC of N2O = 105% (least potent, needs supplementation). Isoflurane MAC = 1.15%, sevoflurane = 2%, desflurane = 6%. Why A wrong: MAC is NOT a safety margin. MAC-BAR (1.5× MAC) blocks autonomic responses. Why C wrong: MAC describes potency, not toxicity. Why D wrong: MAC is a standardized ED50, not peak level. Clinical relevance: Factors decreasing MAC: elderly, hypothermia, opioids, pregnancy, hyponatremia. Factors increasing MAC: hyperthermia, chronic alcohol, amphetamines, young age. Exam trap: 1.3× MAC = ED95 (prevents movement in 95%). MAC is additive when combining agents (0.5 MAC isoflurane + 0.5 MAC N2O = 1 MAC total). Memory aid: 'MAC = Minimum Anesthetic Concentration (50% don't move).' Reference: Aitkenhead – Inhalational Agents.

questions.optionB: Burns >24 hours old, crush injuries, denervation injuries, and hyperkalemia (risk of fatal hyperkalemic cardiac arrest due to upregulated extrajunctional acetylcholine receptors)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Succinylcholine causes potassium release — dangerous when extrajunctional AChRs are upregulated. Why correct: Succinylcholine (depolarizing NMB) normally raises K+ by 0.5 mEq/L. In burns (>24h), crush injury, denervation, immobilization, muscular dystrophy → proliferation of extrajunctional nicotinic receptors → massive K+ efflux → fatal cardiac arrest. Why A wrong: RSI is a key INDICATION for succinylcholine (fastest onset — 60 seconds). Why C wrong: Brief procedures (ECT, laryngoscopy) are indications. Why D wrong: ECT — succinylcholine is DOC. Clinical relevance: Safe within first 24 hours of burn (before receptor upregulation). Avoid for up to 1 year after injury. Also contraindicated: personal/family history of MH, penetrating eye injury (raises IOP). Exam trap: The timeframe matters — burns <24 hours = safe; burns >24 hours = contraindicated. Memory aid: 'Succinylcholine + Burns/Denervation = Sudden K+ Catastrophe.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Neuromuscular Blocking Agents.

questions.questionText: Sevoflurane is preferred over halothane in children because:
questions.optionB: Non-pungent, pleasant odor allows smooth inhalation induction, and it has less hepatotoxicity and cardiac arrhythmogenicity than halothane
questions.explanation: Core concept: Sevoflurane = ideal inhalation induction agent in children. Why correct: Sevoflurane: non-irritant sweet smell → smooth mask induction in children (no coughing/laryngospasm). Lower cardiac sensitization to catecholamines vs halothane (less arrhythmias). No significant hepatotoxicity (halothane → 'halothane hepatitis'). Why A wrong: Sevoflurane is more expensive than halothane. Why C wrong: Sevoflurane MAC = 2%; halothane MAC = 0.75% — halothane is more potent. Why D wrong: Desflurane has fastest elimination (lowest blood:gas coefficient = 0.42). Clinical relevance: Compound A: sevoflurane + CO2 absorber → potentially nephrotoxic fluoride compound (clinical significance debated — use fresh gas flow >2 L/min). Exam trap: Desflurane has the FASTEST onset/offset but is PUNGENT → NOT suitable for inhalation induction. Memory aid: 'Sevoflurane = Sweet smell = Smooth induction = Standard for children.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Inhalational Agents.
